[[Maria Menounos]]{{cite news|last=Leviste |first=Lanz |title=The King sweeps the Oscars |url=http://www.philstar.com/young-star/242278/king-sweeps-oscars |access-date=July 19, 2013 |work=[[The Philippine Star]] |publisher=PhilStar Daily, Inc. |date=March 12, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140203012124/http://www.philstar.com/young-star/242278/king-sweeps-oscars |archive-date=February 3, 2014 }} }} The '''76th Academy Awards''' ceremony, presented by the [[Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences]] (AMPAS), honored the best [[2003 in film|films of 2003]] and took place on February 29, 2004, at the [[Kodak Theatre]] in [[Hollywood, Los Angeles|Hollywood]], Los Angeles. During the ceremony, AMPAS presented [[Academy Awards]] (commonly referred to as Oscars) in 24 categories. The ceremony, televised in the United States by [[American Broadcasting Company|ABC]], was produced by [[Joe Roth]] and was directed by Louis J. Horvitz. Actor [[Billy Crystal]] hosted for the eighth time. He first presided over the [[62nd Academy Awards|62nd ceremony]] held in 1990 and had last hosted the [[72nd Academy Awards|72nd ceremony]] held in 2000.{{cite news|last=Munoz |first=Lorenza |title=Crystal returns to familiar role |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2003-sep-25-wk-munoz25-story.html |access-date=July 26, 2013 |work=[[Los Angeles Times]] |date=September 25, 2003 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131010082327/http://articles.latimes.com/2003/sep/25/news/wk-munoz25 |archive-date=October 10, 2013 }} Two weeks earlier in a ceremony at [[The Langham Huntington, Pasadena|The Ritz-Carlton Huntington Hotel & Spa]] in [[Pasadena, California]] held on February 14, the Academy Awards for Technical Achievement were presented by host [[Jennifer Garner]].{{cite magazine|last=Susman |first=Gary |title=Garnering Prizes |url=https://ew.com/article/2004/01/29/jennifer-garner-will-host-scientific-and-technical-oscars/ |access-date=July 8, 2013 |magazine=[[Entertainment Weekly]] |publisher=[[Time Warner]] |date=January 29, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131202232147/http://www.ew.com/ew/article/0%2C%2C585283%2C00.html |archive-date=December 2, 2013 }} ''[[The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King]]'' won a record-tying eleven awards including [[Academy Award for Best Director|Best Director]] for [[Peter Jackson]] and [[Academy Award for Best Picture|Best Picture]].{{cite news|last=Morales|first=Tatiana|title=Peter Jackson, Lord Of The Oscars|url=https://www.cbsnews.com/news/peter-jackson-lord-of-the-oscars/|access-date=July 24, 2013|work=[[CBS News]]|publisher=[[CBS Corporation]]|date=February 13, 2009}} Other winners included ''[[Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World]]'' and ''[[Mystic River (film)|Mystic River]]'' with two awards and ''[[The Barbarian Invasions]]'', ''[[Chernobyl Heart]]'', ''[[Cold Mountain (film)|Cold Mountain]]'', ''[[Finding Nemo]]'', ''[[The Fog of War|The Fog of War: Eleven Lessons from the Life of Robert S. McNamara]]'', ''[[Harvie Krumpet]]'', ''[[Lost in Translation (film)|Lost in Translation]]'', ''[[Monster (2003 film)|Monster]]'', and ''[[Two Soldiers (2003 film)|Two Soldiers]]'' with one. The telecast garnered nearly 44 million viewers in the United States, making it the most-watched telecast in four years. ==Winners and nominees== The nominees for the 76th Academy Awards were announced on January 27, 2004, at the [[Samuel Goldwyn Theater]] in [[Beverly Hills, California]], by [[Frank Pierson]], president of the Academy, and the actress [[Sigourney Weaver]].{{cite magazine|last=Susman |first=Gary |title=Three-'Ring' Circus |url=https://www.ew.com/ew/article/0,,574422,00.html |access-date=July 8, 2013 |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |publisher=Time Warner |date=January 27, 2004 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131004214300/http://www.ew.com/ew/article/0%2C%2C574422%2C00.html |archive-date=October 4, 2013 }}{{cite news|last1=Fernandez |first1=Maria Elena |last2=Braxton |first2=Greg |title=Dawn's early rite |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2004-jan-28-et-fernandez28-story.html |access-date=July 19, 2013 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=January 28, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131004215520/http://articles.latimes.com/2004/jan/28/entertainment/et-fernandez28 |archive-date=October 4, 2013 }} ''[[The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King]]'' received the most nominations with eleven; ''[[Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World]]'' came in second with ten. The winners were announced during the awards ceremony on February 29, 2004. With eleven awards, ''The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King'' is tied with ''[[Ben-Hur (1959 film)|Ben-Hur]]'' and ''[[Titanic (1997 film)|Titanic]]'' as the most awarded films in Oscar history.{{cite news|last=Smith |first=Neil |title=Rings joins Oscar's big winners |url=https://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/entertainment/3521401.stm |access-date=July 8, 2013 |work=[[BBC News]] |publisher=[[BBC]] |date=March 1, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131105110109/http://news.bbc.co.uk/2/hi/entertainment/3521401.stm |archive-date=November 5, 2013 }} Moreover, its clean sweep of its eleven nominations surpassed ''[[Gigi (1958 film)|Gigi]]'' and ''[[The Last Emperor]]''{{'}}s nine awards for the largest sweep for a single film in Oscar history.{{cite news|title="Lord of the Rings" Wins 11 Oscars |url=https://abcnews.go.com/Entertainment/Oscars2005/lord-rings-wins-11-oscars/story?id=116560 |access-date=July 9, 2013 |work=[[ABC News (United States)|ABC News]] |publisher=[[The Walt Disney Company]] |date=March 1, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140224002917/http://abcnews.go.com/Entertainment/Oscars2005/lord-rings-wins-11-oscars/story?id=116560 |archive-date=February 24, 2014 }} The film was also the tenth film to win [[Academy Award for Best Picture|Best Picture]] without any acting nominations.{{cite news|last1=Crow |first1=Kim |last2=Vancheri |first2=Barbara |title=Third "Ring" movie wins 11 Oscars |url=http://old.post-gazette.com/ae/20040301oscarfinal0301fnp1.asp |access-date=July 9, 2013 |publisher=[[Crain Communications]] |work=[[Pittsburgh Post-Gazette]] |date=March 1, 2004 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222041100/http://old.post-gazette.com/ae/20040301oscarfinal0301fnp1.asp |archive-date=February 22, 2014 }} Best Director nominee [[Sofia Coppola]] became the first American woman and third woman overall to be nominated in that category.{{cite news|last=Klein |first=Joshua |title=Coppola feeling 'Lost in Translation' |url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/2004/02/03/coppola-feeling-lost-in-translation/ |access-date=July 8, 2013 |work=[[Chicago Tribune]] |publisher=Tribune Company |date=February 3, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222015946/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2004-02-03/features/0402030265_1_sofia-coppola-golden-globes-translation |archive-date=February 22, 2014 }} By virtue of her father, [[Francis Ford Coppola]] and her grandfather, [[Carmine Coppola|Carmine]]'s previous wins, her victory in the Original Screenplay category made her the second third-generation Oscar winner in history.{{cite news|last=Ryzik |first=Melina |title=Oscar's First Family? Depends on How You Count |url=http://carpetbagger.blogs.nytimes.com/2013/01/11/oscars-first-family-depends-on-how-you-count/ |access-date=July 25, 2013 |work=The New York Times |date=January 11, 2013 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130530112507/http://carpetbagger.blogs.nytimes.com/2013/01/11/oscars-first-family-depends-on-how-you-count/ |archive-date=May 30, 2013 }} With [[Sean Penn]] and [[Tim Robbins]]'s respective wins in the Best Actor and Best Supporting Actor categories, ''[[Mystic River (film)|Mystic River]]'' became the fourth film to win both male acting awards.{{cite news|last=McNary |first=Dave |author-link=Dave McNary |title=A 'River' of dreams for Penn, Robbins |url=https://variety.com/2004/film/news/a-river-of-dreams-for-penn-robbins-1117900991/ |work=Variety |publisher=PMC |access-date=August 16, 2013 |date=February 29, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140304222637/http://variety.com/2004/film/news/a-river-of-dreams-for-penn-robbins-1117900991/ |archive-date=March 4, 2014 }} ===Awards===

[[Will Ferrell]] || Performers || "Get Off the Stage" song parody during the Best Original Song presentation{{cite magazine|title=Will Ferrell and Jack Black had a memorable 2004 Oscars moment |url=https://www.ew.com/ew/article/0,,596240,00.html |access-date=July 24, 2013 |date=March 1, 2004 |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |publisher=Time Warner |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131003050915/http://www.ew.com/ew/article/0%2C%2C596240%2C00.html |archive-date=October 3, 2013 }} |} ==Ceremony information== [[File:Billy Crystal VF 2012 Shankbone.JPG|thumb|upright|[[Billy Crystal]] hosted the 76th Academy Awards.|alt=Photo of Billy Crystal in 2012.]] In light of the record low viewership from the [[75th Academy Awards|preceding year's ceremony]], the Academy sought to make several changes and hire a new producer for the upcoming show. AMPAS announced that unlike previous years where the ceremony typically was held in either late March or early April, the festivities would be held in late February.{{cite news|title=Save the date: Next year's Oscars move to February |url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/2003/03/27/save-the-date-next-years-oscars-move-to-february/ |access-date=July 8, 2013 |work=Chicago Tribune |publisher=Tribune Company |date=March 27, 2003 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131011111343/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2003-03-27/news/0303280034_1_oscar-ballots-spirit-awards-golden-globes |archive-date=October 11, 2013 }} AMPAS director of communications John Pavlik explained that the purpose of moving the telecast a month earlier was "to bolster the ceremony's sagging television ratings and protect the Oscar's status as the nation's pre-eminent awards event."{{cite news|last=Lyman |first=Rick |title=Oscars to Arrive A Month Earlier Starting in 2004 |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2002/08/05/movies/oscars-to-arrive-a-month-earlier-starting-in-2004.html |access-date=July 19, 2013 |work=[[The New York Times]] |date=August 5, 2002 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131011111726/http://www.nytimes.com/2002/08/05/movies/oscars-to-arrive-a-month-earlier-starting-in-2004.html |archive-date=October 11, 2013 }} Despite several Academy officials denying such reasons, some industry insiders speculated that the earlier Oscar date was also implemented to mitigate the intense [[For Your Consideration (advertising)|campaigning and lobbying]] during Oscar season put forth by film studios.{{cite news|title=Decision 2004|url=http://www.thedailybeast.com/newsweek/2003/03/12/decision-2004.html|access-date=July 19, 2013|work=[[Newsweek]]|publisher=[[The Daily Beast]]|date=March 12, 2003|archive-url=https://archive.today/20130719204006/http://www.thedailybeast.com/newsweek/2003/03/12/decision-2004.html|archive-date=July 19, 2013|url-status=dead}} This marked the first time since the [[14th Academy Awards|14th ceremony]] that the awards were held outside the aforementioned time frame.{{cite web|last=Sheehan |first=Paul |title=Live Blog: The 83rd Annual Academy Awards |url=http://www.goldderby.com/news/1222/live-blog-the-83rd-annual-academy-awards.html |work=Gold Derby |access-date=July 19, 2013 |date=February 27, 2011 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222184339/http://www.goldderby.com/news/1222/live-blog-the-83rd-annual-academy-awards.html |archive-date=February 22, 2014 }} In August 2003, the Academy hired film producer [[Joe Roth]] to oversee production of the ceremony. The following month, Roth recruited veteran Oscar host [[Billy Crystal]] to emcee the awards gala for the eighth time.{{cite news|last=Grossberg |first=Josh |title=Oscar Brings Back Billy Crystal |url=http://www.eonline.com/news/45908/oscar-brings-back-billy-crystal |access-date=July 19, 2013 |work=[[E!]] |publisher=[[NBCUniversal]] |date=September 24, 2003 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131011121811/http://www.eonline.com/news/45908/oscar-brings-back-billy-crystal |archive-date=October 11, 2013 }} To stir interest surrounding the awards, Roth produced three trailers promoting the ceremony that each was set to different pop tunes ([[Madonna (entertainer)|Madonna]]'s "[[Hollywood (Madonna song)|Hollywood]]", [[OutKast]]'s "[[Hey Ya!]]", and [[Pink (singer)|Pink]]'s "[[Get the Party Started]]"). The trailers contained clips of previous ceremonies with slogans such as "Expected the unexpected" and "It's Oscar night" occasionally flashing between scenes.{{harvnb|Pond|2005|p=357}} These promotional spots were shown at movie theaters, on several cable channels, and at participating [[Blockbuster LLC|Blockbuster]] stores.{{harvnb|Pond|2005|p=356}} The Academy also granted talk show host [[Oprah Winfrey]] unprecedented access to rehearsals and meetings as part of a month-long series on her [[The Oprah Winfrey Show|eponymous talk show]] covering behind the scenes preparation of the telecast. ===MPAA ban on screeners=== In September 2003, the [[Motion Picture Association of America]] (MPAA) initially banned distribution of [[screener (promotional)|screener]]s to awards groups, citing fears of [[Copyright infringement|piracy]].{{harvnb|Pond|2005|p=358}} Many independent film studios and prominent film directors objected to this decision charging that this would hurt smaller films for Oscar consideration since they heavily rely on screeners to lure Academy members' attention. The following month, AMPAS and the MPAA reached an agreement in which Academy members would receive the screeners on the condition that they keep them out of reach from people unaffiliated with AMPAS.{{cite news |last=Friedman |first=Roger |title=TV's 'Ed' Won't Sing for Charity |url=https://www.foxnews.com/story/tvs-ed-wont-sing-for-charity/ |access-date=July 29, 2013 |work=[[Fox News]] |publisher=[[21st Century Fox]] |date=October 22, 2003 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131011130245/http://www.foxnews.com/story/2003/10/22/tv-ed-wont-sing-for-charity/ |archive-date=October 11, 2013 }} In December 2003, a federal judge in New York overturned the ban citing that it violated [[United States antitrust law|federal antitrust laws]].{{Cite news|url=https://www.nytimes.com/2003/12/09/movies/while-they-can-studios-rush-to-send-videos-to-oscar-voters.html |title=While They Can, Studios Rush to Send Videos to Oscar Voters |last=Waxman |first=Sharon |work=The New York Times |date=December 9, 2003 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20160717121630/http://www.nytimes.com/2003/12/09/movies/while-they-can-studios-rush-to-send-videos-to-oscar-voters.html |archive-date=July 17, 2016 }}{{harvnb|Pond|2005|p=359}} ===Box office performance of nominated films=== At the time of the nominations announcement on January 27, the combined gross of the five Best Picture nominees was $638 million with an average of $127 million per film.{{cite news|title=2003 Academy Award Nominations and Winner for Best Picture |url=https://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?view=&yr=2003&p=.htm |access-date=July 24, 2013 |work=[[Box Office Mojo]] |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130515042513/http://boxofficemojo.com/oscar/chart/?view=&yr=2003&p=.htm |archive-date=May 15, 2013 }} ''The Lord of the Rings: The Return of the King'' was the highest earner among the Best Picture nominees with $338.3 million in domestic box office receipts. The film was followed by ''Seabiscuit'' ($120.2 million), ''Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World'' ($85.3 million), ''Mystic River'' ($59.1 million), and finally ''Lost in Translation'' ($34.8 million). Of the top 50 grossing movies of the year, 45 nominations went to 10 films on the list. Only ''Finding Nemo'' (1st), ''The Lord of the Rings: Return of the King'' (2nd), ''Pirates of the Caribbean: The Curse of the Black Pearl'' (3rd), ''Seabiscuit'' (16th), ''Something's Gotta Give'' (21st), ''The Last Samurai'' (23rd), ''Master and Commander: The Far Side of the World'' (31st), ''Brother Bear'' (32nd) ''Cold Mountain'' (37th), and ''Mystic River'' (46th) were nominated for Best Picture, Best Animated Feature, or any of the directing, acting, or screenwriting.{{cite news|title=2003 Domestic Grosses |url=https://boxofficemojo.com/yearly/chart/?asof=2004-01-27&view=releasedate&view2=domestic&yr=2003&sort=gross&order=DESC&p=.htm |access-date=February 18, 2014 |work=Box Office Mojo |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222190043/http://boxofficemojo.com/yearly/chart/?asof=2004-01-27&view=releasedate&view2=domestic&yr=2003&sort=gross&order=DESC&p=.htm |archive-date=February 22, 2014 }} ===Tape delay implementation=== In light of the [[Super Bowl XXXVIII halftime show controversy|controversy]] surrounding the [[List of Super Bowl halftime shows|halftime show]] during [[Super Bowl XXXVIII]], network [[American Broadcasting Company|ABC]] implemented a five-second [[Broadcast delay|tape delay]] to ensure that profanity and obscenity were not seen or heard.{{cite news|last=Carey |first=Matt |title=ABC to impose delay on Oscar telecast |url=http://www.cnn.com/2004/SHOWBIZ/Movies/02/05/sprj.aa04.abc.oscar.delay/ |access-date=July 25, 2013 |work=CNN |publisher=Time Warner |date=February 5, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222133201/http://www.cnn.com/2004/SHOWBIZ/Movies/02/05/sprj.aa04.abc.oscar.delay/ |archive-date=February 22, 2014 }} AMPAS president [[Frank Pierson]] protested this decision in a written statement, stating, "Even a very brief tape-delay introduces a form of censorship into the broadcast—not direct governmental control, but it means that a network representative is in effect guessing at what a government might tolerate, which can be even worse."{{cite news|last=Cadorette|first=Guylaine|title=Academy Miffed about Oscar Tape Delay, Black Eye for the Nerdy Guy, DJ Rick Dees Ends Morning Radio Show, More...|url=http://www.hollywood.com/news/brief/1742769/news-feb-11-academy-miffed-about-oscar-tape-delay-black-eye-for-the-nerdy-guy-dj-rick-dees-ends-morning-radio-show-more?page=all|access-date=July 29, 2013|work=[[Hollywood.com]]|publisher=Hollywood.com, LLC|date=February 11, 2004|archive-date=May 12, 2022|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20220512083909/https://www.hollywood.com/news/brief/1742769/news-feb-11-academy-miffed-about-oscar-tape-delay-black-eye-for-the-nerdy-guy-dj-rick-dees-ends-morning-radio-show-more?page=all|url-status=live}} In response, producer Joe Roth reiterated that censorship would only be applied to profanity and not political speeches.{{cite magazine|last=Susman |first=Gary |title=Snippy Response |url=https://ew.com/article/2004/02/11/academy-blasts-abc-over-oscar-tape-delay/ |access-date=July 29, 2013 |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |publisher=Time Warner |date=February 11, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140222132814/http://www.ew.com/ew/article/0%2C%2C20356896_589703%2C00.html |archive-date=February 22, 2014 }} ===Critical reviews=== The show received a mixed reception from media publications. ''[[Chicago Tribune]]'' television critic Steve Johnson lamented that the show "felt almost numbingly familiar and disappointingly genteel." He also criticized broadcaster ABC's decision to implement the five-second tape delay.{{cite news|last=Johnson |first=Steve |title=Crystal's no ball in tepid Oscar show |url=https://www.chicagotribune.com/2004/03/01/crystals-no-ball-in-tepid-oscar-show/ |access-date=July 9, 2013 |work=Chicago Tribune |publisher=Tribune Company |date=March 1, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150411031637/http://articles.chicagotribune.com/2004-03-01/features/0403010015_1_oscars-new-tune-academy-awards |archive-date=April 11, 2015 }} [[Tom Shales]] of ''[[The Washington Post]]'' quipped that the ceremony "was about as entertaining as watching [[Jell-O]] congeal." He also added that the lack of surprises among the awards contributed to the dull atmosphere of the telecast.{{cite news|last=Shales |first=Tom |title=A Clean and Boring Sweep |url=https://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/articles/A18433-2004Mar1.html |access-date=July 22, 2013 |newspaper=[[The Washington Post]] |date=March 1, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150413073703/http://www.washingtonpost.com/wp-dyn/articles/A18433-2004Mar1.html |archive-date=April 13, 2015 }} Columnist Tim Goodman of ''[[San Francisco Chronicle]]'' bemoaned, "The 76th annual Academy Awards dragged on without much drama or comedy, sucking the life out of the event even while it was doing justice to the masterpiece that is ''The Lord of the Rings''."{{cite news|last=Goodman |first=Tim |title=Unlike 'Lord of the Rings,' this year's Oscars were a yawner – with good ads |url=http://www.sfgate.com/entertainment/article/Unlike-Lord-of-the-Rings-this-year-s-Oscars-2815989.php#page-1 |access-date=July 29, 2013 |work=[[San Francisco Chronicle]] |publisher=[[Hearst Corporation]] |date=March 1, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150411113047/http://www.sfgate.com/entertainment/article/Unlike-Lord-of-the-Rings-this-year-s-Oscars-2815989.php |archive-date=April 11, 2015 }} Other media outlets received the broadcast more positively. [[Ken Tucker]] of ''[[Entertainment Weekly]]'' praised Crystal's hosting performance saying that he "has located the perfect middle ground between [[Steve Martin]]'s adroit silliness and [[Whoopi Goldberg]]'s unapologetic hamminess." On the show itself, he said that it "managed to do what Hollywood may not have: convince us that this was a great year for the movies."{{cite magazine|url=https://www.ew.com/ew/article/0,,597949,00.html |title=The Show |date=March 12, 2004 |first=Ken |last=Tucker |magazine=Entertainment Weekly |publisher=Time Warner |access-date=July 9, 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131011134619/http://www.ew.com/ew/article/0%2C%2C597949%2C00.html |archive-date=October 11, 2013 }} Film critic [[Andrew Sarris]] of ''[[The New York Observer]]'' wrote that the show was "the funniest and least tedious in memory." He also extolled producer Joe Roth by concluding, "As far as this old critic's concerned, Mr. Roth, you did a fine job."{{harvnb|Pond|2005|p=384}} ''[[USA Today]]'' critic Robert Bianco commented that despite the lack of suspense due to the ''Lord of the Rings'' sweep of the awards "Crystal was able to lace funny bits throughout the evening." He further lauded the show as "more glamorous and upbeat than last year's war-muted event, and decently paced."{{cite news|last=Bianco |first=Robert |title=Crystal's return enlivens predictable show |url=http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2004-03-01-bianco-review_x.htm |access-date=July 8, 2013 |work=USA Today |publisher=Gannett Company |date=March 1, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20140228202936/http://usatoday30.usatoday.com/life/movies/movieawards/oscars/2004-03-01-bianco-review_x.htm |archive-date=February 28, 2014 }} ===Ratings and reception=== The American telecast on ABC drew in an average of 43.56 million people over its length, which was a 26% increase from the previous year's ceremony.{{Cite web |first=Bill |last=Gorman |title=Academy Awards Averages 41.3 Million Viewers; Most Since 2005 |url=http://tvbythenumbers.com/2010/03/08/academy-awards-averages-41-3-million-viewers-most-since-2005/44217 |work=[[TV by the Numbers]] |publisher=Tribune Company |date=March 8, 2010 |access-date=March 12, 2010 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20100310080531/http://tvbythenumbers.com/2010/03/08/academy-awards-averages-41-3-million-viewers-most-since-2005/44217 |archive-date=March 10, 2010 |url-status=dead }} An estimated 73.89 million total viewers watched all or part of the awards. The show also earned higher [[Nielsen ratings]] compared to the previous ceremony with 26.68% of households watching over a 41.84 share.{{cite news|title=Academy Awards ratings |work=Television Bureau of Advertising |url=http://www.tvb.org/media/file/Academy_Awards.pdf |access-date=June 27, 2013 |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130515152338/http://www.tvb.org/media/file/Academy_Awards.pdf |archive-date=May 15, 2013 }} In addition, it garnered a higher 18{{ndash}}49 demo rating with a 15.48 rating over a 38.79 share among viewers in that demographic. It was the highest viewership for an Academy Award telecast since the [[72nd Academy Awards|72nd ceremony]] held in 2000.{{cite news|last=McDaniel |first=Mike |title=Significant ratings rise for Oscars |url=https://www.chron.com/entertainment/article/Significant-ratings-rise-for-Oscars-1961268.php |date=March 2, 2004 |access-date=July 24, 2013 |work=[[Houston Chronicle]] |publisher=Hearst Corporation |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131005004535/http://www.chron.com/entertainment/article/Significant-ratings-rise-for-Oscars-1961268.php |archive-date=October 5, 2013 }} In July 2004, the ceremony presentation received nine nominations at the [[56th Primetime Emmy Awards|56th Primetime Emmys]].{{cite news|title=Primetime Emmy Award database |url=http://www.emmys.com/shows/76th-annual-academy-awards |access-date=July 24, 2013 |work=[[Academy of Television Arts and Sciences]] |publisher=ATAS |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20130622210234/http://www.emmys.com/shows/76th-annual-academy-awards |archive-date=June 22, 2013 }} Two months later, the ceremony won one of those nominations for Louis J. Horvitz's direction of the telecast.{{cite news|title=The complete list of winners |url=https://www.latimes.com/archives/la-xpm-2004-sep-20-et-emmylist20-story.html |access-date=July 15, 2013 |work=Los Angeles Times |date=September 20, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131011141213/http://articles.latimes.com/2004/sep/20/entertainment/et-emmylist20 |archive-date=October 11, 2013 }}{{cite news|title=2004 Emmy Winners |url=https://www.nytimes.com/2004/09/20/arts/television/20emmylist.html |access-date=July 25, 2013 |work=The New York Times |date=September 20, 2004 |url-status=live |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150610235809/http://www.nytimes.com/2004/09/20/arts/television/20emmylist.html |archive-date=June 10, 2015 }} =="In Memoriam"== The annual "In Memoriam" tribute was presented by Academy President Frank Pierson.
